items.q: What is the difference between monetary and fiscal policy?
items.correct: Monetary policy uses interest rates and money supply controlled by the central bank; fiscal policy uses government taxation and spending controlled by the Treasury.
items.misconception: Monetary policy is about money and fiscal policy is about taxes.
items.random_wrong: Both terms mean the same thing — government economic policy.
items.why: While the rough distinction has some truth, the key analytical point is WHO controls each (independent central bank vs elected government) — central to debates on policy independence.

items.q: What determines the size of the fiscal multiplier?
items.correct: Mainly the marginal propensity to consume (MPC) — the higher the share of extra income people spend domestically, the bigger the multiplier. Leakages (saving, imports, taxes) reduce the multiplier.
items.misconception: The fiscal multiplier is always 2 or 3.
items.random_wrong: The fiscal multiplier is the rate of growth of the money supply.
items.why: The multiplier varies with conditions — bigger in slumps with high MPC and slack capacity, smaller at full employment or in open economies. Students learn a fixed number rather than the determining mechanism.

items.correct: An initial injection of spending circulates as recipients spend portions, generating more income — total effect on GDP is larger than the initial injection.
items.misconception: The multiplier means government spending is always good because it multiplies wealth.
items.random_wrong: The multiplier is the rate at which interest compounds.
items.why: The multiplier is a mechanism, not a verdict. Whether to use it depends on conditions (slack in economy, marginal propensity to consume). Students confuse the descriptive concept with a policy prescription.
The answer examiners want is: Fixed yearly pay. “Hourly pay” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Pay based on what you sell is…
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Commission. “Bonus” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Hourly wage £10, work 35 hours. Gross weekly pay, in £?
items.why: Exact answer: 350. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
